data.title: CoreCivic: A Dividend Play In 2026
data.description:
Introduction

Since my last article on CoreCivic (NYSE:CXW), where I explained that analysts' fears about the stock were unwarranted and recommended a "buy opinion," the stock went up from $17-$18 to around the low $20s per share.

Over the past few months, the demand for detention beds has increased dramatically, leading to the reactivation of several idled facilities by CoreCivic, which contributed to its strong Q2 results. Furthermore, on July 4th, Congress passed the "OBBB" One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which significantly increases funding for ICE, allocating $45 billion to expand detention capacity through September 2029, intended to fund approximately 100,000 beds, up from the 41,500 funded since late 2024.

The increasing demand for detention beds from CoreCivic's federal customers and the reduction in capital expenditures may lead to stronger cash flows in 2026. That would allow the company to issue dividends and allocate additional capital returns to shareholders.

Company overview

CoreCivic is the nation's largest owner of partnership correctional, detention, and residential reentry facilities and one of the largest operators of such facilities in the United States. Through its three segments: CoreCivic Safety, CoreCivic Community, and CoreCivic Properties the company provides a broad range of solutions to government partners. Source: (Company's 2024 annual report).

As of June 30, 2025, CoreCivic owned and managed in the Safety segment 44 facilities with a total designed capability of over 67,000 beds. If we also add the last facility they recently bought in July, the total bed capacity would be 68,000 beds.

In the Community segment, the company operated 21 facilities with a designed capacity of roughly 4,000 beds. For what concerns the Property segment, CoreCivic owned 5 facilities with a designed capacity of more than 7,000 beds.

CoreCivic generates its revenues from contracts awarded by its government partners. Half of what they make comes from federal contracts by US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and US Marshals. The rest of the revenue is generated by the State partners.

Q2 2025 results

CoreCivic recently released its results for the second quarter of 2025, significantly surpassing analysts' estimates.

The company reported revenue of $538.2 million, up 9.8% from the prior-year quarter, and net income of $38.5 million with EPS of $0.35, doubling the previous year's results. The primary contributor to these strong results was the increasing demand from ICE, reaching new all-time highs in detention populations.

Furthermore, management raised its guidance for the full year 2025, forecasting EBITDA of approximately $370.0 million, net income of $120.0 million, and FFO of about $2 per share.

Share Repurchase and M&A activities

During the second quarter, CoreCivic bought back 2.0 million shares at an aggregate cost of $43.2 million, meaning they purchased shares at an average price of approximately $21.50 per share. This shows that the management is bullish on the stock. They repurchased the same number of shares in the previous quarter. If they continue repurchasing shares at this pace, this would result in a capital return for shareholders of over $160.0 million per year, or approximately 7% of the current market capitalization. As of June 2025, they still have roughly $240.0 million available for buybacks out of the $500.0 million share repurchase program.

At the beginning of July, they completed the acquisition of the Farmville Detention Center, a 736-bed facility, for a total purchase price of $67.0 million. The facility is expected to generate annual incremental revenue of approximately $40.0 million.

Facilities Activated

Over the past few months, CoreCivic has reactivated several facilities across the country to meet ICE's needs. The latest was the West Tennessee Detention Facility earlier this month. The company has made available to ICE over 8,000 beds, including those from the recently acquired Farmville Detention Center (Facilities Contracted and Under Letter Contract in the chart below).

If those facilities were at full capacity, they could generate approximately $500.0 million in additional revenue per year, based on an estimated $50,000 revenue per inmate, as stated by CoreCivic and The GEO Group (NYSE:GEO) management during conference calls. Management said that it will take time to ramp up these facilities, forecasting full capacity in 2026.

Regarding the two facilities under letter contract, CoreCivic is working to negotiate and execute long-term contracts, having received funding to help cover start-up expenses. However, the intake process for the Midwest Regional Reception Center in Leavenworth, Kansas, has been delayed due to a lawsuit filed by the City of Leavenworth. The company is appealing the temporary restraining order, and management is confident about successfully resolving the dispute.

With respect to the idled facilities, if they were to be reactivated, CoreCivic could generate up to $500.0 million in additional revenue, on top of the half a billion already mentioned. Given the high demand for detention beds by ICE and the increase in the U.S. Marshals population, the company is well positioned to meet its customers' needs and capture more federal contracts.

The table below shows the number of people detained by ICE since Trump took office. As of August 10, ICE detained nearly 60,000 people and is forecasted to reach 100,000 by year-end.

Valuations

The valuation has not changed significantly since my last article. This time, I forecast the revenue they are likely to generate for this year and 2026 based on the contracts they have secured. Then, I made conservative assumptions for 2027 through 2029, assuming they will obtain additional contracts in the future.

From 2026 onward, I raised the EBITDA margins because the company is expected to incur fewer G&A expenses (such as charges associated with the acquisition of new facilities or expenses related to debt payments and refinancing transactions), and the occupancy bed rate is likely to improve as operations ramp up.

If my estimates are correct, CoreCivic should be able to generate more than $250.0 million in free cash flow (FCF) by 2026, representing a P/FCF ratio of 9 based on the current market value. In 2025, cash flows are significantly lower due to higher capital expenditures related to the cost of reactivating facilities and acquisitions. Further in the analysis, I will explain how management could deploy these cash flows and why I believe CoreCivic could be a dividend play from 2026 onward.

In my model, I discount the future cash flows by 10%, which is my target rate of return. I calculated the terminal value by applying a multiple of 15 to the free cash flow of the last estimated year. I believe this multiple is fair, given the company's future growth outlook and low leverage. Finally, I subtract the value of long-term debt ($1 billion) from the present value of cash flows, arriving at a valuation of approximately $3 billion, or $28 per share.

Dividends

During the conference call, several matters arose. In particular, an analyst asked about initiating a dividend in 2026. Management replied that at current stock prices, they would not do so, suggesting that the stock is significantly undervalued. On a personal note, I agree with management. If the stock price were to trade at current levels in 2026, I would continue buying back shares rather than paying a dividend. However, I am confident that moving forward, the market will fairly evaluate the stock.

If the cash flows approach the levels I previously mentioned, management could allocate $100 million to dividends (which would equate to a 4.5% dividend yield), $100 million to buybacks, and the remainder to paying down debt, resulting in a capital return to shareholders of more than 10%. Furthermore, this move might attract dividend funds, and the stock could be included in some dividend ETFs, potentially pushing the stock price higher.

Conclusions

CoreCivic is experiencing a highly positive period with unprecedented growth opportunities. Management's ability to operate efficiently has positioned the company in a strong financial state, generating robust cash flows that they have been allocating effectively to enhance shareholder returns through acquisitions and buybacks. Their capability to be effective capital allocators, combined with the strong growth they are experiencing, may enable them to generate substantial cash flows as early as 2026, potentially allowing them to issue dividends. This move could place the stock in a favorable position, attracting the interest of dividend funds, which could ultimately drive the stock price higher.

data.title: Energy Fuels Has The Assets, But Not Yet The Numbers
data.description:
Energy Fuels Inc. (NYSE:UUUU) is a company with a story that grabs attention. It sits at the center of two industries that governments care a lot about right now: uranium for nuclear power and rare earths for advanced technology. The company has been trying to move beyond being just a uranium miner and instead become one of the few Western firms with real rare earth separation capacity. That ambition has been enough to spark investor excitement, and the share price has climbed about 93 percent over the past year, reaching a three-year high in August 2025.

The problem is that the financial results are not keeping up with the promise. In the second quarter of 2025, Energy Fuels generated only $4.2 million in revenue while spending more than $30 million on operations. That translated into a net loss of $21.8 million. Analysts expect more losses through 2025, with the first modest profit not showing up until 2026. To me, that makes the gap between the exciting story and the actual earnings too wide for a confident Buy at this price.

I think the stock already reflects a lot of hope that uranium output will keep climbing and that rare earth production will take off quickly. The long-term upside is there, but with heavy spending and dilution along the way, the risks in the near term are just as obvious. For that reason, I believe the most sensible stance right now is to hold the stock rather than chase it higher.

What the Company is Building

Energy Fuels is best known for its White Mesa Mill in Utah, which processes uranium and also has rare earth separation capabilities. In the last few years, the company has moved aggressively into rare earth projects across the globe, including in Brazil, Madagascar, Kenya, and Australia.

On the uranium side, the company has been able to keep its output strong. The Pinyon Plain uranium mine has been performing well and even set record output. Another important detail is the balance sheet. Energy Fuels carries no debt and holds close to $200 million in cash and securities. That gives it room to keep pushing forward with big projects.

The challenge is that all of these investments are expensive. Administrative costs nearly doubled in the most recent quarter, and operating expenses far exceeded revenues. Energy Fuels is still in the stage where costs are growing faster than output, which puts pressure on margins and tests investor patience.

The Industry Setting

The backdrop looks good for both sides of the business. Uranium demand is rising as more countries turn to nuclear power as part of their energy transition. Rare earths, meanwhile, are gaining attention as a strategic resource since they are needed for electric vehicles, wind turbines, and defense systems. Governments want supply chains that are not reliant on China, which still dominates the industry.

This is where Energy Fuels has an edge. Its separation capacity in the United States makes it a rare partner for big companies. Recent agreements with Chemours and Posco show how valuable that position can be. Still, competition could increase as larger firms decide to enter the market. If that happens, margins could get squeezed, especially if Energy Fuels has not scaled up fast enough.

Global exposure also cuts both ways. The company's project in Madagascar is moving ahead again after a suspension was lifted, but the local fiscal and political environment is still uncertain. In my view, that kind of risk is hard to avoid in mining, and it adds another layer of volatility.

Looking at the Numbers

The numbers highlight how far the company still has to go. In the second quarter of 2025, revenue dropped to $4.2 million from $8.7 million the year before. The operating loss ballooned to $26.2 million, and the bottom line showed a $21.8 million net loss. For the first half of 2025, revenue was just over $21 million, but operating expenses came to $73.5 million, which left a net loss of $48 million. Margins were barely positive at the gross level and very negative at the net level.

The balance sheet is in better shape. Cash and restricted cash totaled about $92 million, and marketable securities added another $126 million. With no debt, the company has financial flexibility. The cost, however, has been dilution. Shares outstanding rose from 198.7 million at the end of 2024 to 229.4 million by mid-2025, a jump of about 15 percent in only six months.

Looking ahead, revenue is expected to grow significantly. Estimates call for $44 million in 2025, then $172 million in 2026 and $258 million in 2027. Profitability does not show up until 2026, and even then, earnings are forecast at only 13 cents per share. That is a thin profit outlook relative to a share price above $9.

What the Valuation Tells Us

At about $9.5 per share, Energy Fuels has a market cap of roughly $2 billion. Since earnings and EBITDA are still negative, price-to-earnings or EV/EBITDA ratios do not offer much insight. The price-to-sales multiple, though, tells a clear story. Based on 2025 revenue estimates, the forward ratio is roughly 45 times sales. Even if revenue rises sixfold by 2027, the multiple would still sit around eight times. That is well above the one- to two-times range seen in uranium peers or specialty chemical firms.

In simple terms, the market already assumes that Energy Fuels will succeed in ramping up production and expanding rare earth output. Unless results come in stronger than current forecasts, the valuation leaves little room for error.

How the Market Reacts to News

The share price has been moving in step with headlines. When the company announced it had started producing rare earths in July 2025, investors piled in and the stock jumped. Earlier reports of strong uranium output added more fuel to the rally, and the approval of its Australian joint venture only reinforced the positive mood.

That enthusiasm cooled when the quarterly results came out and missed both revenue and earnings targets. It was a sharp reminder that the company is still a long way from turning a profit. Right now, the market seems willing to celebrate big milestones while brushing off the weak numbers, but that attitude could flip if projects face delays or if commodity prices drop.

Risks to Watch

The road ahead is far from smooth. Building up uranium and rare earth production takes a lot of capital and precision, so there is plenty of execution risk. Investors also have to deal with dilution since the company has been raising cash by issuing new shares. On top of that, the prices of uranium, vanadium, and rare earths can swing wildly, which makes revenue less predictable. Projects in countries like Madagascar, Kenya, and Brazil bring in political and regulatory risks that could slow progress. Finally, the stock trades at a premium valuation, which leaves little room for disappointment if growth does not measure up.

What Lies Ahead

Analysts expect profits to show up in 2026 as uranium mines increase their output and rare earth sales begin to matter more. If things go according to plan, revenue could grow several times over in the next couple of years. Support from the U.S. government, in the form of subsidies or supply agreements, could also strengthen the case for growth.

In the near term, though, losses will likely continue and shareholders may face more dilution. The key signals to watch are whether rare earth oxide production can move from pilot scale to steady output and whether the company can land long-term contracts with big customers. Those steps would give investors much more confidence in the growth story.

Possible Paths for the Stock

If things go badly, the stock could slip to $5 or $6, especially if commodity prices weaken or projects stall, which would bring more losses and dilution. In a more balanced scenario, the stock stays in the $9 to $12 range, with revenue climbing but profits staying thin. On the upside, the stock could push past $15 if uranium markets strengthen, rare earth production ramps up quickly, and government contracts provide extra support.

Final Take

Energy Fuels is aiming to become a key player in North America's uranium and rare earth supply chains. The long-term story is attractive, and the demand for what the company is producing is not in question. The challenge is that the share price has already run far ahead of the fundamentals. Losses are still large, revenue is modest, and continued dilution makes it costly for investors to stick around.

That is why I believe a Hold rating makes the most sense. If you already own the stock, it is worth keeping for its long-term potential, but I would not be putting in fresh money at today's price. A pullback into the $7 to $8 range, or visible signs of real profitability, would make for a stronger entry point.

This remains a company with a compelling vision, but it has a lot to prove before it can be seen as a reliable leader in critical minerals. The next year or two will be the real test of whether Energy Fuels can make that leap.

data.title: Forward Air: A Special Situation To Consider
data.description:
Since April, Forward Air Corporation (FWRD) shares have surged by over threefold, with most of the gains for FWRD stock accruing this summer, as the logistics company has yielded to shareholder activist pressure, by putting itself up for sale.

So far, this pursuit of "strategic alternatives" has resulted in several private equity firms bidding for the company. The question now, for new and existing investors alike, is whether there's the opportunity for another bite of the apple.

That is, is the potential upside from this special situation already reflected in the share price, or could investors buy in now, only to see their positions gain once again, within a relatively short time frame?

At first glance, it may seem as though risk/return is no longer in one's favor, that the potential additional gains from this company being sold at premium to the current stock price are outweighed by the risk of either the private equity bidders backing out, or the company and its major shareholders deciding that "going it alone" remains the best move.

However, diving deeper, one can make the argument that FWRD, while not yet a merger arbitrage situation, and thus far riskier, may be well worth it, given what potential buyers may be willing to pay, and why the major shareholders may be motivated to sell/rollover their equity into a new, privately-held Forward Air.

FWRD Stock: Background

The full story with Greenville, Tennessee-based Forward Air goes back to the 1990s, but what's most relevant to investors in this special situation goes back to August 2023, when Forward Air first announced plans to merge with privately-held competitor Omni Logistics.

Almost immediately, the market was skeptical of the potential cost and growth synergies, and reacted negatively to the deal announcement, with FWRD stock plunging over 25% on the deal announcement.

Major FWRD shareholders, including ClearBridge Investments and Ancora Holdings, voiced their criticism of the proposed $3.2 billion transaction. This led to Forward Air announcing in October 2023 that it was considering a termination of the merger agreement.

However, Forward Air's attempt to appease shareholders failed to work: Omni Logistics sued when Forward tried to terminate the agreement. After that, the litigation was settled, the deal terms were amended, and in January 2024, the transaction was completed. Between tweaks to the deal terms, coupled with Forward Air's falling stock price, the final purchase price for Omni worked out to be around $2.1 billion.

Yet while the transaction price fell significantly between deal announcement and deal close, the situation with FWRD continued to worsen. Weak results for Q1 2024, coupled with downbeat guidance on account of "weak freight demand," let to another major drop for Forward Air shares.

With FWRD's severe slide from over $100 per share, to between $15 and $25 per share in the months of following the deal close, ClearBridge and Ancora, along with another activist shareholder, Irenic Capital, began pushing for Forward Air to consider a sale of the company.

Later in 2024, Alta Fox, another activist fund, entered a position in FWRD, and started pushing for the same thing as well. Forward Air responded to the activist pressure at the start of 2025, with the announced launch of a strategic alternatives review. Unfortunately, instead of this news giving way to a FWRD recovery, shares instead continued to slide lower, although mostly in reaction to poor quarterly results, as well as on concerns about the impact of tariff hikes on revenue.

However, after briefly falling below $10 per share, since then FWRD stock has experienced a tremendous run-up in price. In May, Ancora announced plans to campaign against the reelection of three board members, including Chairman George Mayes.

This campaign succeeded, and pretty soon, Forward Air started making progress with its "strategic alternatives" initiative. On June 13, the company disclosed that major buyout firms like Blackstone (BX) and Apollo Global Management (APO), as well as firms like Platinum Equity and Clearlake, had all signed NDAs, indicating their interest in conducting due diligence for a potential buyout offer.

By July, Forward Air announced that five private equity firms have so far made bids for the company, and on Aug. 18, Axios Pro reported that second-round bids from these potential buyers are due by early September.

Valuation: How Much is Private Equity Willing to Pay for Forward Air?

Following FWRD stock's rapid rise back to the low-$30s per share, the company's valuation has obviously increased substantially as well. At today's prices, Forward Air trades at a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of around 12.6.

The company currently has a $950.6 million market cap, about $2.22 billion in outstanding debt, and around $95 million in cash, giving FWRD an enterprise value of around $3.08 billion. Based on the forward valuation, Forward Air is expected to generate around $244.4 million in EBITDA this year.

At first, this valuation may appear to be at the high end of what buyers will be willing to pay for an air freight and logistics company. There are several similar names out there trading at EBITDA multiples in the high single-digits, like Radiant Logistics (RLGT) and Hub Group (HUBG).

Still, while at the higher end of valuation, Forward Air isn't the priciest such name out there. GXO Logistics (GXO) trades at a forward EBITDA multiple of 13.8x. C.H. Robinson Worldwide (CHRW) trades for 17.9 times forward EBITDA.

That's not all. Given that the logistics industry may be at the trough of the business cycle, it's perhaps fair to use current valuations to assess how much private equity could be willing to pay. Back in 2023, prior to the industry downturn, and prior to the Omni deal, a pre-merger Forward Air was generating annual EBITDA of around $287 million, while Omni's annual EBITDA was coming in at around $181 million.

Not even taking into account possible cost synergies, rehitting the combined figure of Forward Air and Omni's past EBITDA, $468 million, would represent a tremendous level of improvement over current results.

Add in other factors, like the possibility of Ridgemont Group, which became a 15.25% shareholder after the Omni merger, rolling over its equity into the new privately-held Forward Air, and I believe the potential buyers may be willing to pay a moderate amount extra for this business.

At a valuation of between 13x and 14x EBITDA, the deal price would be at an enterprise value of between $3.2 billion to $3.4 billion. Net of debt and operating lease liabilities, this amounts to between $1.08 billion and $1.28 billion, or between $35 and $41.50 per share, or between 13.4% and 34.5% above the current FWRD stock price.

A Favorable Risk/Reward Setup

Considering how quickly a formal deal could be announced, even if a winning bid for Forward Air is within the low range of my valuation estimate, this would still represent a fairly large return relative to time frame.

Of course, after discussing potential upside, what is the potential downside from entering or maintaining a position in FWRD stock at current prices? With much of the stock's run-up in price driven by rising speculation about an impending sale, it's reasonable to say that, if a deal fails to emerge, FWRD could fall substantially.

Perhaps not all the way back down to $10-$15 per share, but a retreat down to the $20s would be very likely. That said, although the potential for a deal not to emerge is possible, is it probable?

The activists involved with this stock have been more concerned with Forward Air pursuing "strategic alternatives" rather than executing a multi-year turnaround of the business. This points to these activists backing a deal.

Other major shareholders, such as Ridgemont, may be less willing to sell at the low range of my estimated deal price for Forward Air. However, besides the potential for Ridgemont to partner with the private equity bidder, considering how there has already been a bidding round, and the "strategic alternatives" process has progressed, it's questionable that Ridgemont, at this point in time, will suddenly say "no dice" to a takeover deal.

As for other possible scenarios, like one where private equity is interested in buying certain pieces of Forward Air's overall business, rather than the business entirely, I believe that such an outcome will result in a big drop for FWRD stock. In fact, it could spark another rally, as it could pave the way for Forward Air to use the proceeds to de-lever, then proceed with a turnaround that pays off once the macro backdrop for logistics becomes more favorable.

In short, I believe the risk/reward proposition here is very favorable.

Bottom Line on FWRD Stock

As seen from the latest price action with FWRD, dissemination of this special situation appears to be spreading widely. Previously, there may have been growing doubts that Forward Air was on the verge of being sold, but following the Axos scoop, this sentiment is changing:

It's unclear how much longer FWRD will be in the high-$20s or low-$30s, but if one is able to accumulate a position at such prices, this could serve as a worthwhile near-term wager on further "strategic alternatives" progress.

Existing shareholders may want to continue holding on as well, rather than selling into this latest rally. At or near current prices, the potential remaining upside appears to outweigh the risk that deal talk with FWRD stock fully fizzles out.

data.title: Kingsoft Cloud: Revenue Beat And Favorable Outlook Support A Buy
data.description:
I've remained bullish on Kingsoft Cloud Holdings (NASDAQ:KC) (3896.HK). My opinion of its Q2 2025 showing and H2 2025 prospects is positive.

KC's CapEx guide and client mix were the focus of my previous September 11, 2024 article.

Bottom Line Adjusted For One-Offs Exceeded Expectations

The group issued a 6-K filing revealing its latest quarterly financials last week. KC's underlying performance was much better than what headline numbers suggest.

It recorded a Q2'25 net loss of -CNY 300M on a non-GAAP basis. This was wider than the sell-side's projection of -CNY 242M as per S&P Capital IQ data.

KC highlighted an "increase of credit loss resulting from prepayments made to suppliers related to the procurement of certain servers" in its results release.

The firm's normalized bottom line for the April-June period this year is estimated to be an above-consensus -CNY 0.17B, excluding that non-recurring item. It's also an improvement from losses of -CNY 301M and -CNY 191M for 2Q24 and Q1'25, respectively.

KC had witnessed a more rapid pace of growth and substantial margin enhancement.

I wrote in my Sep. '24 update that its "top-line performance, in particular its AI revenue, will most probably be strong going forward." I took into account the fact that "the bulk of the company's capital expenditures" will be "AI-related."

KC's sales linked to AI as a proportion of its group-level top line rose by 4ppts (percentage points) QoQ and 14ppts YoY to 31% in Q2 2025.

The company noted at its analyst briefing that it assists customers to "leverage sophisticated AI models and platforms without need for extensive in-house infrastructure" as a cloud vendor. It's easy to understand how the firm's results have been boosted by the GenAI wave.

Its overall top line growth accelerated from +3% for Q2 2024 and +11% for Q1 2025 to +24% for the recent quarter. This was equivalent to a +6% beat.

KC also saw its Q2'25 EBITDA-to-sales metric widen by +1,410bps (basis points) year-on-year and +110bps sequentially to 17%. The group explained in its earnings release that "the expansion of AI business with higher margin" was a profitability driver.

I think that its outperformance can be replicated in the quarters ahead, as detailed in the subsequent section.

Anticipating a Brighter Future

At the Q2 earnings call, management guided that "the growth rate for revenue" in H2'25 will "be significantly better and higher than the first half of this year." The firm's "margin level" is also expected to "stabilize at where we are right now today," as per the earnings meeting commentary.

My take is that the company is too conservative about its margin prospects. I do agree that its top line should be expanding faster in the latter half of '25.

AI-related tailwinds mentioned in the preceding section should still be a major growth engine for KC. The other thing to watch is the financial contribution from its key clients, Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF) (OTCPK:XIACY) and Kingsoft Corporation (OTCPK:KSFTF).

According to S&P Capital IQ data, KSFTF and XIACF own 47% and 14% of KC's shares, respectively. The latter stressed at its Q2 2025 briefing that it is the "sole strategic cloud platform of the Xiaomi and Kingsoft ecosystem."

KC's revenues generated from KSFTF/XIACF surged +70% YoY in Q2'25 to account for 27% (Q1'25: 25%) of its total sales.

XIACY has set a goal to double its companywide "R&D investment" to CNY 0.2T in the next 5-year period. Kingsoft Cloud disclosed that it is "in the process of delivering an even larger cluster for Xiaomi's computing power demand" at the recent quarterly call. KSFTF also indicated at its August 20 call that its productivity software arm "will continue to increase its R&D investments in AI." It is very likely that KC will win more business from these two customers in the foreseeable future.

I'm predicting a +28% YoY rise in KC's H2 2025 top-line, which compares favorably with its actual H1 increase of 18%.

My crystal ball says that the group's EBITDA margin can possibly increase from 17.3% in the latest three-month period to 19% and 22%, respectively.

The growing share of "higher-margin" AI revenue discussed above should still have a positive impact on Kingsoft Cloud's profits.

I also see KC as a beneficiary of fixed-cost leverage as it becomes bigger.

Chinese internet giant Alibaba's (BABA) cloud unit's forecasted FY2025-2027 EBITDA margins are in the 34-40% range. The figures are sourced from Goldman Sachs' (GS) March 17, 2025, report (not publicly available) titled "Addressing Key Debates On Alibaba Cloud." This can be taken as the potential ceiling for KC's EBITDA-to-revenue ratios in the long run.

Variant Views You Should Consider

KC's future revenue could slow if XIACF and KSFTF choose to lower CapEx, especially AI-related spending.

The company might report a weaker-than-expected margin in the quarters that follow. This assumes operating de-leverage materializes because of growth moderation.

My Conclusion

I'm sticking to a Buy for KC.

The firm should be able to outperform in H2 2025, judging by the encouraging read-across from Q2 results.

A valuation rule of thumb states that a stock's earnings multiple should be roughly equal to its expected growth rate. In that respect, KC's EV/EBITDA has the potential to expand by +80% (27/15) over the next three years. Its consensus FY25-27 EBITDA CAGR estimate and forward next twelve months' EBITDA ratio are +27% and 15x (source: S&P Capital IQ), respectively.

data.title: IDEX: A Wonderful Business Finally Trading At A Real Undervaluation
data.description:
Investment Thesis

IDEX Corporation (IEX) continues to be a lovely mid-cap specialised industrial company that, despite a softer demand environment, is producing growth and value, albeit just at a slightly slower pace than before.

Second quarter results were mostly positive, in my opinion, with the firm doing more of the same good business as in previous quarters. While demand and thus growth may remain curtailed over the coming quarters, I think that the long-term profile of IDEX remains unchanged.

Importantly, the valuation has become much more attractive since my last analysis in February, with shares now trading at what could be a 20% undervaluation.

I accordingly have decided to upgrade my rating for the firm from a Hold to a Buy given the more lucrative pricing of shares and my continued admiration for the company as a whole.

Business Overview & Economic Moat

Business Profile

IDEX is a diversified industrial manufacturing company that produces thousands of niche yet mission critical industrial components ranging from pumps, valves, and films to Jaws of Life rescue tools and specialty medical materials.

Through their Fluid and Metering (F&M), Health and Science (H&S) and Fire, Safety and Diversified products (FS&D) segments, IDEX has focused on narrow segments of each of these industries in which they have obtained a dominant market position.

I wrote a detailed analysis into the three key business segments as well as the entire business profile as a whole back in February, and I recommend you read that deep-dive article here to get a real understanding of what it exactly is that IDEX does, as their operational profile is quite vast.

Economic Moat

As a quick recap, the provision of niche, mission-critical components to industries in which failure is unacceptable is a superb business strategy in my opinion, as it creates moatiness for the firm in multiple ways.

The first and most important is pricing power. By being the primary supplier in a majority of their different product segments, IDEX is able to command higher prices for their products, as they are often one, if not the sole producer.

Compounding this pricing power is the recurring revenue IDEX is able to obtain from customers, as not only do they sell these components in the first place, but they then provide the maintenance and replacement services too.

For a customer to replace an IDEX part or system with one from a competitor would require significant capital and time, which is often not realistic nor profitable for mission-critical processes. As a result, IDEX’s customers face real switching costs should they want to swap over to a competing company’s products.

Finally, I think that the reputation IDEX and their multiple brands have garnered within their respective industries is highly valuable as it fosters positive relationship building with customers. While it would also be expensive for clients to switch from IDEX products to those of competitors, I don’t believe there is a real incentive for them to do so thanks to the reputation IDEX components have for reliability and quality.

This only makes it even more difficult should a rival firm try to vie customers away, as it would require them to overcome what I consider to be powerful and long-standing business relationships.

These factors all combine to create a wide economic moat for the company, which protects their returns and margins from competitive erosion.

Fiscal Analysis – Q2 2025 Update

Second Quarter Results

July 30 saw IDEX report their second quarter results, which I found to be positive given what has become a slowing industrial demand backdrop in the U.S. and abroad.

The key driver of growth at IDEX continues to be their rapid mergers & acquisition strategy, which has seen the company acquire multiple smaller manufacturers and integrate them quickly and efficiently into their operational processes.

Net sales increased a healthy 7% YoY overall thanks to robust demand primarily for their H&S Technologies, which actually saw organic sales growth of 4% (20% YoY including new acquisitions).

While new acquisitions such as that of Micro-LAM, a company specialised in difficult-to-machine forming capabilities (essentially using diamond turning machines and laser heating to cut specialty components), would do little on their own to move the needle at IDEX, the trick lies in how they can integrate this new skill and share knowledge between subsidiaries.

By being able to share capacity, skills, and technology from across their portfolio of businesses, IDEX can significantly increase the technological capabilities of individual units while simultaneously rapidly innovating within different product categories.

I think it is this exact ability to rapidly add processes and products to their portfolio that enables IDEX to continue producing growing sales despite a slowing macro environment.

Management shares this sentiment and noted that even within the still growing H&S segment, key end markets such as semiconductor manufacturers were experiencing slowing demand compared to previous periods.

Softer demand was the key reason for a decline in net sales within the F&M segment too, with a flatline demand environment in diversified industrial and agricultural segments not being offset by increased pricing power and volume deleverage.

Finally, the smallest FS&D segment actually saw some rather healthy growth thanks to an increased demand for IDEX’s more comprehensive “integrated solutions”.

What this means is that customers are committing more to entire systems produced by IDEX rather than just individual products or components, which nicely supports my earlier hypothesis about customer relationships being largely positive and repeat sales growing at the firm.

The cumulative effect of their strong H&S and FS&D performance along with slightly flatline results at the F&M segment was a still respectable 2.7% YoY increase in operating income, leaving the company with a solid Q2 operating margin of 22%.

This margin expansion came despite an 11% increase in SG&A expenses, which I am surprised by considering the new acquisitions made by the firm and the time it takes to fully integrate these units into their operations.

Management also reiterated that demand has softened faster than expected in 2025, most likely due to uncertainty around future macroeconomic policies present in the U.S. This has impacted key demand areas such as the semiconductor and auto industries particularly harshly.

Nevertheless, Seeking Alpha’s Quant appears to agree with my sentiment that the firm remains a nice and profitable operation with the “B+” letter grade potentially being excessively swayed by the low CAPEX/Sales and ATR ratios.

I find this to be quite normal considering the rapid rate at which the firm is growing through acquisitions, with key indicators such as their 14% net margin, 8.1% ROIC, and gross margin of 45% being simply excellent even on an absolute basis.

Balance Sheet & Cash Flows

On the balance sheet front, I have little to report as the firm remains an incredibly well capitalised, stable, and economically astute business.

Debt to equity remains incredibly low at about 0.5x, which is phenomenal to see considering how quickly the firm continues to acquire new businesses.

Interest coverage remains strong at 11x while their leverage is comfortably below 2x (a benchmark I find to be generally sound for most profitable and value producing businesses) at around 1.7x.

The firm continues to hold about $568 million in cash on their balance sheets, which combined with TTM's unlevered FCF of $532 million illustrates quite nicely how well IDEX’s core operations can produce cash for the firm.

An important comparison I like to do is between FCF per share versus EPS, which in the case of IDEX in Q2 is $7.35 in FCF compared to $6.19 in earnings.

A larger FCF per share than EPS illustrates the quality of IDEX’s earnings, with real cash being produced continuously from operations, which the firm can then use for whatever business processes they deem fit.

The significant cash flows should allow the firm to continue their strong share repurchase program, which saw IDEX buyback $50 million worth of shares in Q2 with similar plans for Q3 and Q4.

Long-term debentures also reduced by $100 million in Q2 as the firm continued to repay maturities as they arise with little to no real concern for their ongoing business processes.

FY25 Outlook & Beyond

Considering all of the qualitative and quantitative performance achieved by IDEX in Q2, I remain quite happy with the business as a whole. Their core strategy of purchasing value-add business units, which can then be rapidly integrated into the IDEX portfolio of products, continues to generate solid results for the firm.

While the short-term outlook for FY25 was toned down from a previous 1-3% revenue growth estimate to just 1%, along with a drop in EPS to around $7.90 from $8.00, I think the long-term outlook for IDEX remains unchanged.

Valuation

Since my last article in February, the valuation of IDEX has become quite a lot more attractive overall, with the Seeking Alpha Quant letter grade dropping from a “D-“ to a “C-“.

Relative Valuation Metrics

On the valuation metric side of things, the firm’s P/E GAAP TTM has dropped from 30x to 26x while their P/S has fallen from a rather pricey 4.4x to a more reasonable 3.7x.

P/CF TTM has also reduced from 19x to 17x, which, along with the other contracting ratios, has come due to a pullback in investor sentiment surrounding the firm’s more flatline YoY Q2 earnings figures.

A look at the Seeking Alpha Advanced Chart for IEX confirms this claim, with shares generally underperforming the market (as represented by the S&P 500 tracking SPY (SPY) ETF) by a pretty large 32%.

While not ideal for existing shareholders, I think this pullback in the share price has created a better valuation for the company, which considering their long-term outlook may mean that an undervaluation could be present.

DCF Modelling Update

To confirm this, I updated my DCF valuation with the new share price data and financial information that I got from Seeking Alpha’s Financials tab for IEX stock.

I slightly adjusted my projected future growth rate down from 8% to 7.8% to reflect what I expect to be a slowdown in the coming year or two due to macroeconomic uncertainty and the time it takes for industrials to begin expanding again after a period of soft demand.

My terminal growth rate remains at 3%, as I don’t foresee IDEX becoming that much bigger of a component of the U.S. economy as a whole, and my 8% discount rate continues to be based on the 7.98% annualised average return produced by the S&P500 over the last 94 years.

I use this rate as my discount factor, as it nicely reflects the opportunity cost I am faced with when choosing to invest in any given company instead of in the market as a whole.

With these inputs, I got a new IV per share figure of $204, which, compared to my previous February IV number of $207, remains essentially unchanged. However, relative to the current share price, the valuation has gone from fairly valued to what is now a 20% undervaluation.

Short-term (1-12 month) returns are very difficult to predict, and once again, I will simply decline to make one. However, I think the long-term (1-10 year) potential for IEX shares to markedly outperform is real, as the business is growing rapidly and generally very good at creating value.

IDEX Corporation Risk Profile

I still think the firm faces risk from two key areas: market cyclicality and competitive forces.

Market Cyclicality & Demand

The impact a declining and flatline business cycle will have on earnings is exactly the concept currently placing a damper on the firm’s earnings.

Industrial end markets and industries such as semiconductors, automotive applications, and agricultural segments can be slow to pick up in up-cycles too so I suspect this very risk will continue to weigh on earnings for another 4-5 or so quarters, depending on what happens in the U.S. and global economies as a whole.

Competition & Expansion

Operationally IDEX must continue to innovate within their respective product categories in order to stay ahead of their rivals from a capability perspective. I think that while their long-standing customer relationships provide moatiness in the mid-term (about 3-8 years), long-term success will hinge on the firm’s products still being the best performers within their segments.

To do this, IDEX will have to continue acquiring smaller business units at hopefully great prices that can be quickly integrated and create sustainable value for the company as a whole.

I don’t think that the company has any real concerns at present that go beyond the short-term profitability threat posed by cyclicality, with management continuing to exhibit astute management of the business as a whole.

With this in mind, I continue to rate the firm as having a medium risk profile, although I still recommend all readers and investors conduct their own research into these matters before making a decision.

Summary

IDEX continues to be a profitable company that is able to generate both growth and cash from operations even despite a softer end-market demand environment.

Second quarter results saw the firm doing more of the same business they always have (acquiring smaller units and growing sales), albeit simply at a slightly slower clip than previously.

The biggest development since my last article is certainly the valuation, which has gone from fairly priced to a more attractive 20% undervaluation. As such, I have upgraded my rating from a Hold to a Buy at present time to reflect the greater opportunity I see for outsized returns to be made from holding IEX shares.

data.title: Global Industrial Reframes Growth Strategy Amid Tariff Pressure
data.description:
GIC Focuses on Strategic Accounts

I have been discussing Global Industrial Company (NYSE:GIC) in the past, and you can read the latest article here, published in December 2024. In the middle of 2025, GIC is prioritizing specialization and expansion, aiming to deepen relationships with higher-value customers while reducing reliance on price-sensitive segments. I think this strategy should enhance profitability and long-term stickiness.

Revenue growth is being led by large strategic accounts, supported by modest pricing actions. However, tariffs pose a risk that will require careful supplier diversification and cost management. Margins hit record highs in Q2, but sustaining them will depend on offsetting inflationary pressures. Strong cash flow, a debt-free balance sheet, and a healthy dividend place the company well for steady EBITDA growth ahead. The stock appears undervalued compared to its peers. However, it has rallied 32% in the past month. So, given the lack of any imminent impetus, I suggest investors continue to "Hold" the stock.

Why Do I Keep My Rating Unchanged?

In my last iteration on GIC, published on December 23, 2024, I wrote:

GIC continues refining its strategies, shifting its focus from a serve-all policy to building customer relationships. It has started rolling out Salesforce (a new CRM) to strengthen business-to-business relationships. It expands new product categories and verticals and converts select items into private labels. The industry environment appears to have improved in the recent past, although it is still not expansionary.

After Q2, GIC is focusing on targeted customer growth and broader product expansion to drive profitability while reducing exposure to price-sensitive segments. Strong Q2 results reflected record margins and growth from strategic accounts, supported by pricing actions and disciplined cost control. However, tariff pressures remain a risk. Given the relatively cheap valuation multiples, I continue to rate it a "hold."

Explaining Outlook

After Q2, GIC plans to anchor growth on specialization and expansion, focusing on targeted customer acquisition and deeper engagement with existing clients. I think reframing the go-to-market approach should help attract higher-value customers. At the same time, it plans on avoiding over-reliance on price-sensitive segments. It will look to expand into broader product categories, aiming to drive profitable growth.

The company’s US and Canadian sales both expanded in Q2, with July carrying the momentum through “mid-single-digit growth,” estimates the company’s management. Strategic accounts continued to gain traction, while the management decided to scale back promotional activities targeting lower-value customers to focus on margin accretion. Pricing actions helped offset volume pressure. However, tariffs remain a risk. It will require ongoing supplier diversification and effective cost management from GIC to counter the margin pressure.

Market Opportunities

The company is expanding its assortment and SKUs, which is helping sustain growth momentum. I think its focus on deeper customer understanding should strengthen alignment and delivery. Over time, the addressable market is expected to broaden beyond infrastructure into wider industrial equipment and MRO supplies.

Industry Outlook

The U.S. unemployment rate rose slightly year-over-year but remains well below its five-year average. GDP returned to growth in Q1 2025 after contracting in the prior quarter. However, the ISM Manufacturing PMI stayed below 50 for the fifth month, signaling ongoing industrial contraction despite stronger production.

My Estimates

Over the past 16 quarters, GIC's adjusted EBITDA increased by 6%, on average. Given the tariff impact and the company’s cost reduction initiatives, I expect the EBITDA to secure a similar growth trajectory while its topline growth stabilizes. Over the next four quarters, I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase by 5%–7%. Given its trailing 12-month EBITDA of $96 million, I expect the adjusted EBITDA to grow to ~$102 million in the next four quarters.

Q2 Results Analyzed

Year-over-year, GIC's revenues increased by 3.2% in Q2 2025, as disclosed in the earnings announcement on July 29. A strong momentum in large strategic accounts primarily led the revenue growth, while smaller transactional customers softened. Gross margin reached its highest since Q2 2022, aided by modest price capture and favorable FIFO timing. However, it appears that part of this benefit is temporary. Operating income and margin also hit recent highs, reflecting disciplined execution and proactive cost control.

Margin Drivers and Outlook

In Q2, GIC’s gross margin rose to 37.1% - a 180 basis point jump over the previous year. Better price capture, FIFO inventory timing, and lower freight costs aided the improvement. However, it appears tariff-driven inflation is starting to weigh on sequential trends. Its SG&A expenses held flat at 27.7% of sales, reflecting disciplined cost control. I think the margin sustainability will depend on how effectively management offsets tariff pressures while maintaining supply chain flexibility.

Cash Flows and Debt Level

In 6M 2025, GIC's cash flow from operations (or CFO) increased by 39% compared to a year ago due to favorable changes in inventory balances and accounts receivable. Free cash flow (or FCF), excluding acquisitions, improved by 46% in the past year.

GIC's liquidity totaled $170 million as of June 30, 2025. It has no debt, unlike some of its peers. It pays a quarterly dividend of $0.26 per share, which equates to a 2.9% forward dividend yield, higher than its peer FAST (1.8% dividend yield).

Relative Valuation and Target Price

GIC's forward EV/EBITDA multiple versus the current EV/EBITDA is expected to contract more sharply than its peers. This indicates that its EBITDA is expected to increase more sharply than its peers in the next four quarters, typically resulting in a higher EV/EBITDA multiple. Its EV/EBITDA multiple (14.9x) is lower than its peers' (FAST, DNOW, DSGR) average. So, the stock is undervalued compared to its peers.

If the stock trades at the five-year average (13.5x), it can decrease by 5%. If the stock trades at the peers’ average (18x), the stock price can increase by 27%. Since my last publication in December 2024, where I suggested a "hold," the stock has increased by approximately 43%.

As I discussed above, I expect 5%-7% adjusted EBITDA growth in the next four quarters. Feeding these values into the EV calculation and assuming the current EV/EBITDA multiple holds, I think the stock should trade between $35 and $36, implying an unchanged stock price in the following year.

Wall Street Rating

One analyst rated GIC a "hold," according to Seeking Alpha. None rated it a "sell" or a "buy." The consensus target price is $38, suggesting a 7% upside at the current price. Given the current value drivers, Wall Street analysts are reasonable with their returns.

What's The Take On GIC?

GIC is sharpening its growth strategy by focusing on specialization, targeting higher-value customers, and expanding into broader product categories. I think this shift away from price-sensitive segments should strengthen profitability and customer stickiness.

Momentum in large strategic accounts is supporting steady revenue growth, while pricing actions are helping offset volume softness. However, tariffs remain a key risk, requiring supplier diversification and cost discipline. Margins have reached record levels, but sustainability will hinge on managing tariff-driven inflation. The stock significantly underperformed the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) in the past year. I think EBITDA growth will gradually accelerate, supported by disciplined execution, stronger cash flows, and a debt-free balance sheet. Given the relative valuation multiples and stable value drivers, the stock price can hold steady, validating my "hold" rating.
